---
title: "example_chapter" # make sure this matches the chapter label in main.tex
format:
  latex:
    highlight-style: github # This is specifically for formatting code snippets in this example chapter
    keep-tex: true          # required so that the .tex is produced for each chapter
      
params:
  eval_models: false        # tells knitr to look for cached models. Set to `true` to re-evaluat models
  
knitr:
  opts_chunk: 
    cache_comments: false          # turn off cache comments
    crop: true                     # this is a perl script that crops image pdfs
    out-width: NA                  # for an easier time with figure sizes, let knitr handle these
    out-height: NA                 # as above
    cache.path: "../thesis_cache/" # add in thesis cache directory
    
execute: 
  echo: false       # echo tells knitr whether to display code (not just output)
  warning: false    # display warnings
  message: false    # display messages
  include: false    # whether to include the output of a chunk            
---

```{r}
#| label: setup

# load libraries

library(dplyr)       # for wrangling data
library(readr)       # for reading csv
library(ggplot2)     # for creating example plots
library(kableExtra)  # for working with tables
library(qwraps2)     # for producing the model cache
```

```{r}
#| label: retrieve-cached-models-actual

# load in cached models

if (!params$eval_models){ lazyload_cache_dir("../thesis_cache/") } # conditional cached model loading
```

# Introduction

Welcome to the unofficial University of Manchester Reproducible Thesis Template.
My PhD project was done in an entirely open and reproducible manner, including
all papers and my final thesis. I developed this template in case anyone 
else wanted to have a go at writing a fully
reproducible thesis. I am an R user exclusively, so I wrote my thesis using
Quarto (https://quarto.org/) and the Rstudio (https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/)
Integrated Development Environment, although everything here should also work
with VS Code (https://code.visualstudio.com/). In this example/tutorial chapter, I cover:

 - The general workflow for using the template.
 - How to write using quarto-flavoured markdown.
 - How to use citations, cross-referencing, and labels.
 - How to use code chunks to generate figures and tables, including how to
 use pre-rendered graphics.
 - How to use code chunks for statistical modelling.
 - How to use the scripts provided to render your final document.
 
## A Note for Users of Other Languages

Quarto uses markdown syntax. I use Rstudio as my IDE. `knitr` is the engine that
interprets code chunks when using Rstudio, and it supports a variety of
languages, including R, Python, SQL, JavaScript, and Ruby. For a full list of
supported languages, type the following command:

`names(knitr::knit_engines$get())`

# Workflow

This template has been set up to make it as easy as possible to get started
writing your fully-reproducible thesis. The general workflow is provided below:

 1. Chapters are stored as `.qmd` files in the `chapters_quarto` folder in the
 parent directory. You should write each chapter separately using these files.
 2. Once you are happy with a draft of a chapter, add it to the `% CHAPTERS %`
 list in `main.tex`. @fig-chapters-tex shows how to do this, and it must be done
 for every chapter.
 3. Before producing a final thesis version, make sure to edit `main.tex` with
 your chapters, author information, declarations, abstracts, and acknowledgements.
 4. Finally, use the `finalise_thesis.R` script to render each chapter to a `.tex`,
 reformat the `.tex`, then knit them all together into the final thesis .pdf.

```{r}
#| label: fig-chapters-tex
#| include: true
#| fig-pos: "H"
#| fig-cap: Edit this section in `main.tex` to specify your chapters and chapter labels.

# find png in the supplied graphics folder

# include pre-rendered graphics

knitr::include_graphics(path = "../supplied_graphics/chapters_tex_example.png",
                        dpi = NA) # let quarto/tinytex handle DPI options when using
                                  # include_graphics()
```

# Writing With Quarto Markdown

## Setting Up

To use this template, you should have a few things installed. `tinytex` is what
we will use to render the final thesis. `Quarto` is bundled with
newer versions of Rstudio, but if you do not have it, you should install it. Install
`tinytex` using the following command in the console:

`install.packages("tinytex")`

Now that you've installed the tinytex package, you need to install the TinyTex
engine itself using the following:

`tinytex::install_tinytex()`

The UoM LaTeX thesis template that this template is based on uses LuaTeX, so
we need a couple of additional packages to make sure it all runs as intended. Run
the following commands in the console:

```
tinytex::tlmgr_install("luatex85") # allows us to use luatex
tinytex::tlmgr_install("tex-gyre") # additional font that we need
```
Each chapter must have a YAML header. This specified options that we need to
successfully render each chapter. I recommend using the same YAML header for each
chapter, just for convenience
After the YAML header, the first thing you should have in each
`.qmd` chapter is a `setup` chunk. Each
time any code chunk in the document is manually run, the `setup` chunk is 
automatically run first. In it, you should load in the packages you need,
set up any labelling vectors, and deal with function conflicts however you
normally do. All you need to do is label the chunk `setup`. The `.qmd` for this
tutorial chapter has such a chunk.

## The Basics

Quarto.org provides a great guide (https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html)
to writing with quarto markdown. Ignore the image links, but everything else works
as documented. In brief:

*italics*, **bold**, ***bold italics*** are done like this:

`*italics*, **bold**, ***bold italics***`

superscript^2^ / subscript~2~ are done like this:

`superscript^2^ / subscript~2~`

`verbatim code` is done like this:

````
`verbatim code`

````

Lists, both numbered and unnumbered, use markdown syntax.

````
 - item 1
 - item 2
````

Producing:

 - item 1
 - item 2


And numbered lists:

````
 1. item 1
 2. item 2
````

Producing:


 1. item A
 2. item B
 
Footnotes are fully supported[^1].

[^1]: this is a footnote.

## Citations {#citations}

In this thesis template, numerical citations are used, with a single, long
bibliography inserted at the end. `references.bib` is kept in the parent
directory. In-text citations are done like this:

`...as found in previous work \cite{strain_2023}...`

producing this text:

...as found in previous work \cite{strain_2023}...

## Cross-Referencing

Chapters are referenced as such:

`As we can see in \chap{chap:example_chapter}...`, which produces the following:

As we can see in \chap{chap:example_chapter}...

Sections are referenced similarly, but you need to type the word "Section" in
addition.

`As in, in Section \ref{citations}...` produces the following:

As in, in Section \ref{citations}...

Over the whole thesis, you will probably end up with multiple sections called "Introduction",
"Methods", "Results" etc. If this is the case, you can manually specify the
label for a section like so:

```
# Introduction {#introduction_chapter_2}

# Introduction {#introduction_chapter_3}

```
Which allows you to do the following:

In Section 2.2 as opposed to Section 3.2.

Pages are referenced using `\pageref{}`. This does not automatically include
the word "page", so you need to manually add that in. You can point `pageref{}`
to any chapter, section, figure, or table like so:

```
page \pageref{chap:example_chapter}   # chapter

page \pageref{introduction_chapter_1} # section

page \pageref{fig-example}            # figure

page \pageref{tbl-example}            # table

```
You have two options for referencing figures and tables. If you are referencing
something in the same `.qmd` you are writing in, you can use the `@` symbol like so:

`...as we can see in @fig-example and @tbl-example...`, which produces the following:

...as we can see in @fig-example and @tbl-example...

If you are referencing across different chapters, you need to use native LaTeX
referencing style (the same as we use for sections and pages). When doing this,
you need to manually add in "Figure" and "Table", so writing this:

`...as we can see in Figure \ref{fig-example} and Table \ref{tbl-example}...` produces the following:

...as we can see in Figure \ref{fig-example} and Table \ref{tbl-example}...

# Figures & Tables

For reproducibility, figures and tables should be generated from code using
code chunks.

## Figures

Quarto offers a number of figure options that you might be 
familiar with as a LaTeX user. At a minimum, figures require:

 - A label that begins with `fig-` and contains no underscores.
 - The `include: true` option so that the figure is displayed.
 - A figure caption specified by `fig-cap: caption`
 
I prefer to leave `fig-height` and `fig-width` as `NA` in the YAML, and to instead
only specify the `fig-asp` (aspect ratio) in each code chunk. This ensures that
figures always take up the full text width in the resulting pdf document.

If you have pre-rendered graphics, such as screenshots of experiments or
figures that are taken from other published work, you can use `knitr::include_graphics()`
to include them, just as you would in native LaTeX. Below is a code chunk that
accomplishes this. `eval` has been set to false so that the figure itself is
not displayed.

```{r}
#| label: fig-include-graphics
#| include: true                         # let's the figure produce something
#| eval: false                           # prevents code from actually running
#| echo: true                            # outputs code to pdf
#| fig-cap: Placeholder figure caption.  # placeholder caption that won't be on pdf
#| fig-pos: "H"                          # force figure placement  

knitr::include_graphics(path = "../supplied_graphics/chapters_tex_example.png", dpi = NA)
```

Code chunks can be inserted using a button in the top right corner of the editing
window, or by using `Ctrl+Alt+I`. Have a look at `example_chapter.qmd` in
`quarto_chapters` for a demo of this. @fig-example is produced using the R code
chunk directly above it.

```{r}
#| label: fig-example
#| include: true
#| fig-cap: "An example of a figure."
#| echo: true
#| fig-asp: 0.5

# generate some numbers, make a dataframe

x <- rep(1:10)
y <- runif(10)

df <- tibble(x, y)

# load plotting library, make basic plot

df %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_smooth(span = 0.5, se = FALSE)
```

The YAML for this chapter includes the following option `crop: true`. This instructs
`knitr` to use `pdfcrop` to crop produced pdf images. This requires the
`pdfcrop` package, which is not bundled with `tinytex` by default. Install it with 
`tinytex::tlmgr_install("pdfcrop")`. It also requires a `perl` environment. On
Windows, I use Strawberry Perl (https://strawberryperl.com/), which is easy
to install and set up.

## Tables

Most tables you create will by dynamically generated from models/other stats. Otherwise,
I prefer to manually create a table in a .csv file, store it in the directory,
then read it in and use the `kableExtra` package to format it properly. This is
much quicker and more reproducible than manually creating markdown/LaTeX tables.
As with figures, chunks that produce tables have several requirements:

 - A label that begins with `tbl-` and contains no underscores.
 - The `include = TRUE` options so that the figure is displayed.
 - A figure caption specified by `tbl-cap: caption`
 
 Below is a code chunk and the corresponding table that it produces:
 
```{r}
#| label: tbl-example
#| include: true
#| echo: true
#| tbl-cap: An example of a properly formatted table.
#| tbl-pos: "!ht"

# first, read in the csv that the table data lives in

table_df <- read_csv("../data/table_data.csv") # I use read_csv from the readr package

# next, use kableExtra to format and produce the table

kbl(table_df, booktabs = TRUE)                 # imo booktabs produces the best looking tables
```

`kableExtra` also includes options for different kinds of LaTeX styling. See
the awesome_table_in_pdf guide online (https://cloud.r-project.org/web/packages/kableExtra/vignettes/awesome_table_in_pdf.pdf)
for a full `kableExtra` tutorial.

# Models & Caching

Knitr includes the ability to cache models. This is very useful if your models
are especially complex or you have a very large report. This is demonstrated
below.

```{r}
#| label: model
#| echo: true
#| include: true
#| eval: !expr params$eval_models  # allows us to use the eval_models on line 9 of the YAML
#| cache: !expr params$eval_models # allows us to use the eval_models on line 9 of the YAML
#| results: hide

set.seed(123)                               # Set seed for reproducibility
x <- 1:100                                  # Predictor variable
y <- 2 * x + rnorm(100, mean = 0, sd = 10)  # Response variable with noise

data <- data_frame(x, y)                    # turn into a dataframe

model <- lm(y ~ x, data = data)             # Fit a linear model

summary(model)                              # Summary of the model
```

Setting `eval_models` to `TRUE` in the YAML header means models are evaluated and cached with
every knit. Setting to `FALSE` means that knitr looks for the model in the cache,
so you only have to build it once! I use the `qwraps2` package to lazyload
models in at the start of each chapter that includes modelling. This example
chapter does this (see the `.qmd`), and it is reproduced verbatim below:

```{r}
#| label: retrieve-cached-models
#| include: true
#| echo: true

# load in cached models

if (!params$eval_models){ lazyload_cache_dir("../thesis_cache/") }
```

You should do this for every separate chapter that requires cached models.

# Rendering Your Thesis

This repository includes two `.R` scripts that you can use to render your
thesis. You only need to interact with `finalise_thesis.R`. Open `finalise_thesis.R`
and read the comments there. There are lines of code there to render all chapters
(line 6) or render a single chapter (helpful while writing the thesis, line 10).
After that, use the script to call `reformat_tex.R` (line 15), then use lines 
17 and 18 to use the reformatting script. Once that has all run successfully, you
can use line 24 to render the final thesis pdf.

# What's Next?

To ensure full computational reproducibility, we need to make sure that people 
who want to reproduce the thesis in the future can do so using the same package
versions and computational environment. The best way to do this is using a combination
of the `renv` package and Docker.

`renv` is an R package that allows you to capture packages, versions, and dependencies
in your project. Docker is a piece of software that allows you to package your
project so that people can run it virtually in the future.

## Capturing Dependencies Using `renv`

Once you're happy with your thesis, install the `renv` package using:

`install.package("renv")`

Once you've done that, run the following command in the console:

`renv::init()`

This will create several files and folders. The important one is the `renv.lock`
file, which will be created in the parent directory. This file captures all
packages, versions, and dependencies and makes a snapshot of them. Once you have
the `renv.lock` file, you are ready to move on.

## Using Docker to Create an Image

First, install Docker desktop (https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop/).

Then, write your Dockerfile. For your thesis, the easiest thing to do is to
use the Dockerfile that is included in this template repository. Make sure to
change the version of R you are using (line 2), and the files that are added to the 
container (lines 22-36). Lines 39-64 are additional LaTeX packages that I found
I needed to knit my thesis together within the Docker container. You can leave 
everything else as-is, including the `renv` relevant commands (lines 8-19).

# Closing Remarks

I am not going to pretend that writing a fully-reproducible thesis is as easy as
using something like Overleaf. If you've made it this far, I am assuming that you
are at least motivated to try, good luck! There are idiosyncrasies to writing
like this that are best dealt with as they are encountered. My own thesis was
produced using this template, and for reference can be found on GitHub (add final
link). 

Right now, the biggest issue with this is the lack of convenient real-time collaborative
editing. I tried `trackdown`, but it is fairly clunky. There are probably other, better
ways to do this, but in the end I just sent pdfs to my supervisors and they 
commented on them the normal way. While it isn't ideal, it worked well enough for me.

Special thanks go Alex Casson, who wrote the original University of Manchester
PhD thesis LaTeX template that you can find on Overleaf (https://www.overleaf.com/edu/theuniversityofmanchester#templates).
Since then, modifications to the LaTeX `.cls` file have been made by David
Petrescu and myself.
